speaker: interviewer
text: September 1, 1995, survivor is Magda Vass. This is tape number four. You were telling me how you went to the Hungarian women's camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We got there. And we had a little better surroundings there. There were some flowers around. We got flowers, we-- and there was some-- a few cups and saucers, and what have you. It was an apartment, which was empty. And we put flowers in our room. We started to feel like human beings. And one day, my niece went on the bike to look around. And there was a honk-honk from a jeep. And that was the two guys we met at  the headquarters. They say it was an accident. Well, of course, we have a suspicion they find-- found out where Hungarian-Jewish women are. Of course, he will never admit to that. But-- [LAUGHS] Anyhow, they were there. And they-- they-- they met my niece on the bike. And they came and visited us. And the next day they came. And they brought us everything under the sun, toothbrush-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --toothpaste, soap, dresses, shoes, everything you can imagine. And they came just about every day to visit us. Well, mind, of  course, they fell in love. And they got married in Nuremberg, on Christmas Day. But of course, that went on for a while. Meanwhile, that was in early spring. Meanwhile, I was very anxious to go back to Hungary. And Ike-- that is my niece's husband-- had special papers. He could travel all over Europe. The guy who was  his chauffeur was from Poland. He  was a refugee who came to the United States as a refugee before Hitler occupied Poland. I don't know exactly when he came, but he got to the United States. Of course, he wasn't a citizen. But he spoke five languages. And that helped him to be my-- Ike's chauffeur. Because it was very important, the job, what they were doing, to speak several languages. And they-- they pretend that they are Germans. And they pretend they are this or that. They-- they did fantastic work for the United States government-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --risking their lives many times. Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I said, I have to go back, my husband, my brothers. So they took me to Prague, and they put me on a train, which was horrible, because I could feel antisemitism in the-- on the train. I went to Budapest first. Because the train stopped there. And I had-- I looked up some relatives. And I found out that everybody died in my home, in my family. I was a little later going back than some others, because of [? Yutka ?] and Ike. And they always told me they would take me. Don't trust, they will take me. And that's why I got there a little later, in October. Then I went to my hometown. And close to the-- across the street from the railway station, there's the most beautiful legitimate theater building. And across from that, there is a park around the theater. And across from the theater there is a fence, a wooden fence. And the first thing I see is it said, death on the Jews. When I got back to Hungary, to my hometown, that was the first thing I have seen, death on the Jews. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Well, I was scared. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So of course, I told you before, they didn't let me into my parents' house, or my house. But I found a friend who had an apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: To apartments, it was easier to get back than to a home. And I stayed with her. And I was there a couple of days. I was miserable. I don't know what I'm going to do. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And I went to the cemetery, to my father's grave. And on the way home, honk-honk. And I look and it was Ike and Fred, in a Jeep. He came, really, to take my niece's father out if he lived. But of course, he died. So instead of him, I went back with him. Which Wasn't easy for me to decide. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But I did. It was a very frightening trip on the beach. Because at that time, Russians ruled Hungary. As a matter of fact, that whole gorgeous, beautiful theater building was painted all red, horrible-looking thing. Anyhow, at every border, when we got there, they put about four or five heavy army blankets on top of me. And I-- in the back of the Jeep, I was cooped-- this-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] I was, you know, scooping down. And all these blankets, I just about suffocated there. And they talked to the guards and gave them American cigarettes and Hershey chocolate, and we went. And it was-- [LAUGHS] --wonderful to be able to breathe again. But it happened at every border, until finally I got to Prague, I think, where they had connections. And I slept in somebody's house there. And from then on, it was easier. But until we got to Prague, it was very dangerous, for them too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how I got back. Of course, when I got back, my sister was-- expected her husband to come and not me. I was a big disappointment for her. But anyhow, that's-- after that, my niece got married on Christmas Day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got an affidavit to come to the States from Ike's father, and I gave an affidavit to my sister. And that's how we came to  the States. Meanwhile, we were-- after they left, my niece came on a war bride boat, and he wasn't there. But he had some friends who always looked us up and took care of us. And then, we were put in Munich in a DP camp, where they kind of sorted us out, who can come to the States, who can't. You know, they examined us. They took x-rays of lungs and everything, to make sure that we are healthy. That's where I met Mike, my husband, in Munich, in the chow line, they called it, in the breakfast line. He came to me and he said, you look just like my cousin or something like that. Of course, we just met there. And then he left before I did and he went to Springfield, Illinois, where he had a brother, a medical doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a survivor also, your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from Mauthausen. And I went to Maine, where Ike's-- and her husband lived and still live. And a week later, I went to Boston to work, because they were just starting out. He was a young lawyer. His father was a lawyer, too. And we-- Mike and I, we corresponded. And then one day, I went to Springfield. My sister bought a round-trip ticket for me to make sure I come back. [LAUGHS] But I didn't. I married him. September 7th would be our wedding anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister is still living. She will be 94 in September, this month. She lives in the Horizon Club, if you know what it is. It's a retirement-- Marriott's Horizon Club. It's about five minutes' drive from here for me. Five, six minutes. She's doing well. She's--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were separated at some point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Never? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. I ran after her, remember, when I said she's my mother, too. And I ran out of that barrack, where everybody was killed and I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did she come to the United States with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With me, together. We came together. Ike greeted us in Boston-- no, in New York, at Ellis Island. I worked in Boston. And-- [LAUGHS] --when he left, he gave each of us $20 bills. Although he said, we will be-- I will be waiting for you. And he will find out when we will arrive. But anyhow, to have some money on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the word came in Munich, that whoever is hiding money or jewelry or this or that, they will be examined. It is black marketing and scared the hell out of us. So we went to the toilet and flushed the $40 down in the toilet.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We were very scared. Who would want to risk anything? So anyhow, we arrived and because we had nothing, we stayed one night in a hotel. And then we went to Maine. And my sister went to work, and I went to Boston. I didn't want to live there. And I worked in a family with children for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went to Springfield, and I married Mike, who was working in a publishing company. Because they had a publishing house in Hungary. They were publishing all kinds of country and government papers and books, and things like that. They were the biggest in Hungary. I never knew Mike, but I knew about the company. So of course, printing he couldn't do because he didn't know English. But he knew everything else, as far as that line goes. Because he-- after graduating, his father sent him to Leipzig, which was the greatest place in Europe for printing industry, and studied machinery and everything. And from there, he went to Vienna, to an art school where learned-- he graduated in graphic art. He was really like an artist in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he hated to be in Springfield because the union wouldn't take him in. And he couldn't work without being a member of the union after a while. But the other workers didn't want him around because they had-- as soon as he started to work, they had less overtime.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we lived in a little furnished room with kitchen privileges in Springfield. And the boss came all the time, please don't go. Please don't go, I will arrange it. Because he needed him desperately. He did beautiful artistic work in bookbinding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, his brother had a brother-in-law living in Rockford, Illinois. And we went there to visit on  a weekend. He took us, you know, with the car, Mike's brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, you have to come to-- to Rockford. It's a very rich city, big industry. You should look it over. You can get a job here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went once, and he looked over, went to every printer. They said, we need a bindery to finish up our work. That's wonderful because printing, he couldn't go into it. His English wasn't good enough for it. So we went. We got a loan from my-- from Ike, and from his brother. And I don't know how, but we started a business. We went to Rockford.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- at that time. we went before and we rented a little room which used to be a beauty shop. And we-- you know, beauty shop has different partitions for dying hair or whatever. So we also lived there. We made it in a bedroom. We had a shower in the basement, and we had a little kitchenette. And we-- we opened up a business. But to cut the story short, that was in '49. My son was born in '53. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBBING] And by that time, we were building our home. We built our own home in a beautiful neighborhood close to a brand-new elementary school. We worked very hard. We worked 10, 12 hours a day. But the business was just coming in. We couldn't handle it anymore. We had to go to another place to rent. And we found a building. We bought a building, too, a rundown building in a terrible condition. And when we looked, the-- the roof was leaking. The windows were broken. The building was full with pigeons. But there was a very, very nice Jewish contractor. He said, I will fix it up for you. [SIGHS] Well, it cost $25,000. We didn't have enough, nothing. He says, you pay me every month, what you can. We didn't even have to sign a paper. We were written up in the Rockford paper all the time, by the way. Because when we came it was, at that time, something interesting. And then, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know how or what, but they found out about how our business was growing. We were-- we got a job from a very big publishing company. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't think of the name of the publisher or the place. You know, the-- a publishing company was in a small town, very small town close to Rockford. And they heard about us and they gave us jobs. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we already had a machine for that job, to put little seals. When you get something in the mail, advertising, you know, there's a little seal, something to hold it together. Sometimes, they just put in a-- I can't think now. Anyhow, we got that sealing machine, also so that we pay every month, what we can. It was just a funny thing. It's another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we get in two truckloads of jobs from that-- from that publishing company. First of all, we couldn't put it on the floor. Second thing, if they did, the floor would have down in the ceiling. Because even with one machine my husband worked on, paper cutting-- the heavy machine, cut a bunch of paper at a time-- the floor was moving. So across the street was a very big potato chip factory, Blue Star Potato Chips. And they used to drop in just to talk to us. We was some novelty there, you know? With our broken English and everything. So Mike went over. And it's a big place, big factory, potato chip factory. So Mike went over and I said look, here it's a big job. I don't know what to do with it. Could we bring it over and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --every once in a while, we come and bring just a small portion of the job over to finish? He said, bring over the-- bring over the whole thing. Bring your machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do the whole job here. Just like that. And that's what we did. That job meant an awful lot of money for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's what-- that's-- they-- they took over. They brought a truck, the potato chip factory. They take-- took all the job over and the machine. And when it was all done, it was there. And the publishing company came and took it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't want to accept any money. Mike took over a bottle of whiskey. They didn't want to even accept that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you found that most Americans were very receptive?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were not Jewish people. The contractor was, but these were not Jewish people. There were-- there was a painting company down the street who went all over, to tell people about us, to give us jobs. Then, we got a-- we did all the yearbooks in May. We didn't know what to do first. We were so busy with the school yearbooks, not only in Rockford, but the outstanding areas. Then, we had-- we did a lot of catalogue binding. So that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you find when you first came to the States that people wanted to hear about your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much. Especially-- especially when I got married in Springfield in 1947. My brother-in-law had some friends, you know, doctors. We were invited to dinner. And one woman said, oh, we had our own trouble, too. We couldn't hardly get sugar. I-- I can never forget that. So they really didn't understand. And we didn't go into it. We didn't-- we-- we-- we didn't talk about it too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back, why do you think that you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes, we were smart. Sometimes, we were lucky, very lucky. We were very smart to try to hide, for instance. But we were very lucky to escape. We're lucky. I think my [? finger ?] has a lot to do with it. Because many times, it saved us from starvation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's 50 years since liberation--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what message do you want to leave to future generations about what you learned from your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Try to fight, if you can, so it should never happen again. But sometimes situations as such as we had when it happened, and we were in the ghetto, that we couldn't help it. So be-- the-- you have to work at it during peacetime. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not when it is too late. Now is the time. If anybody has a big mouth with hatred, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you have to stop it while you can. Because it can be too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I want to thank you very much for telling us--

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: --about your life. And we really appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Vass, before we go to your photographs, you said that there was another story you wanted to tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't-- I forgot to mention that-- because you asked me what can I think that I survived? And I said sometimes we were smart. Sometimes we were lucky. And I didn't say, sometimes we were stupid. When we were in Lichtenau, there was, again, a zahlappell. You know, there were always zahlappells.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the captain of the camp came and looked us over. And that was when I was still bandaged up with my finger. And my sister, who is an optimist forever, she always thought something better will happen. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: She think-- we knew it will be selection, by the way, some kind of selection. And of course, she was very tired of that work in that [GERMAN] camp. She says, I bet we are-- you are-- have-- you have trouble here. You could be an invalid. And if-- the invalids will-- to-- will go to another camp. We were told-- we were told that it was separated. People from the ambulance were-- [? ambulance ?] was called the sick room. They were called out. They were sent to a better camp with less work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we are standing in the line, my sister stands up. And she stands-- stands up. We were standing. She said, captain, please, my niece hurt her finger. Maybe we could go to another camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he looked at us, and he called us stupid asses and everything. Because he knew me, and he knew my niece. My niece everybody knew because she was gorgeous. She could have been a beauty queen. And she was young. And she didn't let us go. So those people who went, they were all then sent to Auschwitz. And here, we volunteered.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have another question. Do you think about the Holocaust often now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Especially lately, because of the fifty years, you know. Also, you know, I'm in constant touch with my niece, whom I love and she loves me. We are more like sisters, really. And we talk a lot on the phone. And she's traveling all over the country all the time because she's being invited to speak. She's really tired already. But there is a list there, her schedule was-- that is, I think, for last year. It's unbelievable, what she does. But she does not accept, anymore assignments to Jewish organizations. Because Jews should know it. But like, not long ago, she was in Minneapolis at a Catholic church. She-- she was invited to Alabama, because a-- at a university, because a processor happened to read her book. Her book is the bestseller-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --at the monument in Washington, after Elie Wiesel. I mean, the best-read women-- woman writer. It's a beautiful book. And so it-- it brings back, when she tells me where she went, where she's going, whatever. I-- I am constantly involved with it, although I never talk to anybody around here, and nobody's interested. And a lot of people think they can't go to see because they would be too upset. That upsets me, when somebody says that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that having your sister and your niece helped you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely, each of us. Well, my sister would have never survived without us. But anyhow, it helped each of us.
